calculation_note: U-value for a single homogeneous insulation layer = thermal_conductivity (W/mK) / thickness (m). Example: 100mm PIR (0.022 W/mK) => U = 0.022 / 0.1 = 0.22 W/m²K. air_permeability_m3_per_hour_m2 is the air leakage rate through the material at 50 Pa differential pressure; lower values indicate better airtightness.

materials.name: PIR (Polyisocyanurate) board
materials.notes: High performance rigid foam board commonly used in roofs and walls. Closed-cell structure makes it an effective air barrier.

materials.name: Rock wool (Mineral wool)
materials.notes: Non-combustible, good acoustic performance, used in pitched roofs, walls and floors. Open fibrous structure is not an air barrier; requires separate membrane.

materials.name: Glass wool
materials.notes: Common batt insulation for cavities and lofts. Very open structure — not an air barrier; always pair with airtight membrane.

materials.name: Wood fibre
materials.notes: Breathable, natural fibre used in breathable build-ups and timber-frame insulation. Vapour-open but not an air barrier without a membrane layer.

materials.name: EPS (Expanded Polystyrene)
materials.notes: Rigid foam used externally and under screeds; good compressive properties. Mostly closed-cell; low air permeability.

materials.name: XPS (Extruded Polystyrene)
materials.notes: Moisture resistant rigid foam for below-ground and insulated floors. Fully closed-cell; acts as air barrier.

materials.name: Cellulose (recycled paper)
materials.notes: Blown or loose-fill insulation with good environmental credentials. Loose fill is not an air barrier; dense-pack at ≥55 kg/m³ offers some resistance.

materials.name: Stud (softwood timber)
materials.notes: Typical timber stud used in framed walls; higher conductivity than insulation so affects cavity U-values where timber paths exist.

materials.name: Joist (softwood timber)
materials.notes: Typical softwood floor/roof joist. Higher conductivity than insulation; represents linear/area thermal bridge — use actual joist depth for R calculations.

materials.name: External blockwork (dense concrete block)
materials.notes: Typical dense concrete block used in external walls. Block faces are relatively dense; air infiltration occurs mainly via mortar joints and cracks.

materials.name: Plasterboard (gypsum)
materials.notes: Common internal lining board; thin layers contribute modestly to thermal resistance. Can serve as inner airtight layer when all joints and penetrations are taped and sealed.

materials.name: Plywood
materials.notes: Wood panel material used for sheathing and finishes; thermal conductivity similar to other timber products. Dense cross-laminate structure gives moderate air resistance.

materials.name: Roof tile (clay/ceramic)
materials.notes: Clay or concrete roof tile — heavy, relatively high conductivity; modelled as an external, discontinuous layer. Tiles are lapped (not sealed) so the tile layer is effectively open to air.

materials.name: Roofing felt / bituminous membrane
materials.notes: Underlay membrane beneath tiles or slates; thin layer with modest conductivity. Provides secondary air resistance; laps and fixings should be sealed for airtight performance.

materials.name: Vapour barrier / control layer
materials.notes: Low-permeability polyethylene or foil membrane installed on the warm side of insulation to prevent interstitial condensation. When all laps and penetrations are correctly taped, provides a near-perfect air barrier.

materials.name: Breather membrane (vapour-open, air-tight)
materials.notes: Vapour-permeable but air-impermeable membrane installed on the cold side of insulation. Allows moisture to escape while resisting wind-driven air infiltration. Correctly lapped and taped at all joints it acts as a robust air barrier.

materials.name: Airtightness membrane (high-performance)
materials.notes: High-performance airtightness layer (e.g. SIGA Majrex, Intello Plus, Pro Clima DB+). Designed to achieve Passivhaus envelope leakage targets (≤0.6 ACH₅₀). All laps, penetrations and perimeter junctions must be sealed with compatible tapes.

materials.name: Window - Single glazing
materials.notes: Traditional single-glazed window; very high heat loss compared with modern glazing.

materials.name: Window - Double glazing (<1995)
materials.notes: Older double glazing with narrow air gap and simple sealed units; higher U-value than modern sealed units with low-e coatings.

materials.name: Window - Double glazing (>1995)
materials.notes: Modern double glazing with low-e coating and argon fill; typical for retrofit and new builds.

materials.name: Window - Triple glazing
materials.notes: Triple glazed units with multiple low-e coatings and inert gas fills; best thermal performance for windows.
